title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Volodymyr Mnih, Koray Kavukcuoglu, David Silver, Alex Graves et al.
hook: A neural network learns to play Atari just by watching the screen.
promise: You will understand how Deep Q-Learning lets computers master games using only raw pixels.
prerequisite_note: It helps to know that computers can learn by trial and error, seeking higher rewards.

glossary.plain: A method where an agent learns how valuable each action is in a given situation.
A neural network good at understanding images, using small filters to detect patterns.
A storage of past experiences used to train the network more effectively.
A platform for testing AI agents on classic Atari games.
A strategy that tells the agent which action to take in each situation.
A prediction of expected future rewards for a given state or action.

cards.title: Neural Nets Play Atari
body: Learn how a neural network can teach itself to play video games from scratch.

cards.title: Can a computer watch and learn?
body: Imagine a computer that learns to play games by simply watching the screen and pressing buttons, much like a human child might.
Instead of being told the rules, it figures out, through trial and error, how to win at classic Atari games like Breakout and Pong.

cards.title: How pixels become skill
body: The process begins with the Atari screen, just a grid of colored pixels, streaming into the neural network.
The neural network processes these images and predicts which actions will bring the highest score, then tries them out and learns from the results.
cards.caption: Raw pixels are transformed into game-winning decisions through learning.

cards.title: Pixels to policies: what changed?
body: Before this paper, agents needed hand-crafted features or simple environments. Now, let's compare old and new approaches.
cards.offNote: Developers must design game-specific features. Performance is limited and does not generalize.
cards.onNote: The agent learns directly from pixels, adapting to many games without manual tuning.

cards.title: Let the network remember and learn
body: A neural network, trained with Q-learning, estimates the value of each possible action from the current screen image.
It stores past game experiences in replay memory, randomly sampling them to break up patterns and stabilize learning.

cards.title: How much memory to replay?
body: Replay memory size affects how well the agent learns from past experiences.
cards.low: Learns quickly but may overfit to recent experiences and forget older lessons.
cards.mid: Balances learning speed with a mix of old and new experiences, improving stability.
cards.high: Learns from a wide variety of situations but may take longer to adapt to new patterns.

cards.title: Pick what makes Deep Q-Learning work
body: Select the key components that help the agent learn efficiently from pixels.

choices.label: Convolutional layers
choices.note: Extracts visual features from raw images.

choices.label: Replay memory
choices.note: Stores and reuses past experiences to improve learning.

choices.label: Q-learning algorithm
choices.note: Guides the agent to maximize future rewards.

choices.label: Epsilon-greedy exploration
choices.note: Balances trying new actions and exploiting known good moves.

choices.label: Target network
choices.note: Stabilizes learning by slowly updating target values.

choices.label: Reward signal
choices.note: Provides feedback for the agent's choices.

cards.title: From pixels to high scores
body: Deep Q-Learning outperformed previous methods on six out of seven tested Atari games, often reaching or exceeding human-level scores.
It showed that a single neural network architecture could learn many different games, with no manual changes between them.
cards.caption: Deep Q-Learning set a new standard for general game-playing AI.

cards.title: Why this was a breakthrough
body: This paper proved that deep neural networks could learn complex behaviors directly from high-dimensional sensory input, not just simple data.
It inspired a new wave of research in reinforcement learning, robotics, and AI, leading to advances like AlphaGo and self-driving cars.

cards.title: How Deep Q-Learning learns from pixels

recap.label: Observe
recap.note: Take in raw pixels from the game screen.

recap.label: Predict
recap.note: Estimate action values using a neural network.

recap.label: Act
recap.note: Choose and perform actions to maximize reward.

recap.label: Learn
recap.note: Update the network using replayed experiences.

cards.title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
body: Read the original paper to see how deep learning and reinforcement learning came together to change AI.
